title: Quarto Tips & Tricks
text: This section is for people who already know how to build Quarto documents and want to go further. All three levels of this tutorial are built with the techniques described here.

title: Quarto Tips & Tricks
section: What’s covered — and where to find it
text:
What’s covered — and where to find it



I want to…
Go to




Control output formats, freeze, and profiles
The YML File


Configure advanced sidebar sections and collapsing
The YML File → Sidebar


Customise the site’s look with themes and CSS
The YML File → Styles


Control how code runs (echo, eval, fold, …)
The QMD File


Show content only in certain profiles or formats
The QMD File → Conditional content


Cross-reference figures, tables, and sections
The QMD File → Cross-referencing


Use quizdown, Pyodide, JSXGraph, or py-exercise
Extensions


Serve the same project in multiple languages
Multilanguage Projects


Look something up quickly
Cheatsheet

title: Quarto Tips & Tricks
text:
What you should already know
This section assumes you are comfortable with the following from the previous levels:

Creating a Quarto website, exercise sheet, or book — Getting Started
Running Python in the browser with Pyodide — Extensions
What interactive Quarto output looks like — Example Page

title: The QMD File
text: A .qmd file is a plain text file combining Markdown for prose and code chunks for computation. This chapter covers the options that give you precise control over how code runs and how content is shown.

title: The QMD File
text:
Code chunk options
Every code chunk can be fine-tuned with options written as #| comments at the top of the chunk:

::: {#ce63c222 .cell execution_count=1}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Hello!
```
:::
:::


Option reference








Option
Values
What it controls




echo
true / false / fenced
Show the source code


eval
true / false
Actually run the code


output
true / false / asis
Show the output


warning
true / false
Show warnings


error
true / false
Show errors (instead of stopping)


include
true / false
Include chunk at all (shorthand for echo+output: false)


code-fold
true / false
Collapse code into a toggle


code-summary
string
Label for the fold toggle


fig-cap
string
Caption for figures


fig-align
left / center / right
Figure alignment


label
string (must start with fig- or tbl-)
ID for cross-referencing





Common combinations
Show output only — hide code:
#| echo: false
Show code but don’t run it:
#| eval: false
Collapsible code block:
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show the code"

title: The QMD File
text:
Global chunk defaults
Set defaults for all chunks in _quarto.yml so you don’t repeat yourself:


_quarto.yml

execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false

Override for individual chunks as needed.

title: The QMD File
text:
Conditional content
Show or hide content depending on the active profile or format:

Profile-based content
Use a custom div with a CSS class and conditionally include it:
::: {.content-visible when-profile="int"}
This paragraph only appears in the **intermediate** profile.
:::

::: {.content-hidden when-profile="beg"}
This is hidden in the beginner profile, visible everywhere else.
:::


Format-based content
::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
This only appears in HTML output.
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
This only appears in PDF output.
:::

title: The QMD File
The QMD File
text:
Custom divs and spans
Apply any CSS class to a block of content using Quarto’s div syntax:
::: {.my-class}
This block gets the `my-class` CSS class.
:::
For inline styling, use a span:
This word is [highlighted]{.my-highlight} in the text.

Built-in Bootstrap utility classes
These work out of the box without any custom CSS:



Class
Effect




.text-center
Centre-align text


.text-muted
Grey text


.fw-bold
Bold text


.border
Add a border


.bg-light
Light background


.p-3
Add padding


.mt-4
Add top margin



Example:
::: {.border .p-3 .bg-light}
This box has a border, padding, and a light background — no custom CSS needed.
:::

title: The QMD File
text:
Callout blocks
Quarto provides five callout types out of the box:
::: {.callout-note}
## Optional title
Informational note.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
Practical advice.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
Something to watch out for.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
Critical information.
:::

::: {.callout-caution}
Proceed carefully.
:::
Add collapse="true" to make a callout collapsible:
::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Click to expand
Hidden detail.
:::

title: Extensions
text:
This chapter covers the four extensions used in this tutorial. Each extension is installed once per project with quarto add <name> — the files land in _extensions/ and should be committed to git.
Extensions can be enabled globally in _quarto.yml or per page in the frontmatter:

title: Extensions
text:
quizdown — interactive quizzes
Install: quarto add parmsam/quizdown Source: (Sauer 2023) — github.com/parmsam/quarto-quizdown

# Which file controls global project settings in Quarto?

1. [x] `_quarto.yml`
2. [ ] `index.qmd`
3. [ ] `styles.css`

# Select all valid Quarto output formats.

1. [x] HTML
2. [x] PDF
3. [x] Word (.docx)
4. [ ] Excel (.xlsx)

# Put the Quarto render pipeline in the correct order.

1. Parse `.qmd` source
2. Execute code chunks
3. Convert Markdown to HTML/PDF
4. Apply theme and styles


SettingsQuestion typesImages & code


Global quiz settings go inside a --- block at the top of the quiz:
```{quizdown}
---
shuffleAnswers: true    # default: true — randomise answer order
shuffleQuestions: false # default: false — randomise question order
---
```






Tip



shuffleAnswers: true is the default — answers are shuffled unless you explicitly set it to false.




Single choice — exactly one correct answer:
```{quizdown}
# What is 2 + 2?
1. [ ] 3
2. [x] 4
3. [ ] 5
```
Multiple choice — more than one correct answer:
```{quizdown}
# Which of these are programming languages?
1. [x] Python
2. [ ] Quarto
3. [x] R
4. [x] JavaScript
```


Include an image in a question:
```{quizdown}
# What does this logo belong to?
![](https://quarto.org/quarto.png)
1. [x] Quarto
2. [ ] RMarkdown
```
Include inline code in answers:
```{quizdown}
# Which chunk option hides the source code?
1. [x] `echo: false`
2. [ ] `eval: false`
3. [ ] `output: false`
```

title: Extensions
section: Pyodide — live Python in the browser
text:
Pyodide — live Python in the browser
Install: quarto add coatless-quarto/pyodide Source: (Droettboom et al. 2021) — github.com/coatless-quarto/pyodide

ExampleSetup & optionsvs. regular Python



Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


Enable per page:
---
filters:
  - coatless-quarto/pyodide
---
Or globally in _quarto.yml (applies to all pages):
filters:
  - coatless-quarto/pyodide
Per-chunk options:
```{pyodide-python}
#| autorun: true     # run automatically on page load
#| readonly: true    # reader cannot edit the code
print("Auto-runs, cannot be changed.")
```






{python}
{pyodide-python}




Runs at
Render time
Read time (in browser)


Reader can edit
No
Yes


Needs Python installed
Yes (author)
No (reader)


Output baked into HTML
Yes
No — computed live









WarningPackage availability



Pyodide runs in WebAssembly. Most pure-Python packages work (numpy, pandas, matplotlib) but packages with C extensions may not be available.

title: Extensions
section: py-exercise — editable exercises with hidden tests
text:
py-exercise — editable exercises with hidden tests
Install: quarto add Erasmus-CTM/py-exercise Source: (Erasmus CTM, n.d.) — github.com/Erasmus-CTM/py-exercise






ImportantRequires Pyodide



py-exercise runs student code in the browser using Pyodide. Both filters must be active on the same page — either globally in _quarto.yml or together in the page frontmatter:
filters:
  - coatless-quarto/pyodide
  - py-exercise



ExampleSyntax



  
    Please enable JavaScript to load the interactive exercise.
  




```{py-exercise}
def my_function(x):
    """Docstring shown to the reader."""
    # Student fills this in
    pass

## TESTS ##
assert my_function(2) == 4, "Feedback shown on failure"
assert my_function(0) == 0
```
Rules: 1. Write the function stub with a docstring at the top. 2. ## TESTS ## is the separator — everything below is hidden from the reader. 3. Each assert statement’s message becomes the feedback shown on test failure.






Tip



Write assertion messages as if you are talking to the student: "my_function(2) should return 4 — did you multiply correctly?"

title: Extensions
Quarto Tutorial
text:
This is a Tutorial for using Quarto in a creating context.

If you never saw a Quarto page before click here!

If you want to learn how to create something with Quarto click here!

If you already know how to create Quarto Documents and want to become better click here!

title: Cheatsheet
text: A quick reference for everything covered in this section.

title: Cheatsheet
text:
YAML — _quarto.yml options

Project
project:
  type: website       # website | book | manuscript | default
  output-dir: docs    # where rendered files go


Execute (global code defaults)
execute:
  echo: true          # show source code
  eval: true          # run the code
  warning: false      # hide warnings
  error: false        # hide errors
  freeze: auto        # auto | true | false


Format
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    theme: cosmo
    theme:
      - cosmo
      - custom.scss
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    number-sections: false
  pdf:
    toc: true
    documentclass: article
    geometry: margin=2.5cm
  docx:
    toc: true


Website
website:
  title: "My Site"
  navbar:
    left:
      - text: "Home"
        href: index.qmd
      - text: "External"
        href: https://example.com
    right:
      - icon: github
        href: https://github.com/your/repo
  sidebar:
    - id: my-sidebar
      style: docked        # docked | floating
      contents:
        - href: page.qmd
          text: Page Title
        - section: "Group"
          contents:
            - href: sub.qmd
              text: Sub-page


Profiles
profile:
  default: web
  group: [web, print, de, en]

title: Cheatsheet
text:
QMD — code chunk options
#| echo: false          # hide source code
#| eval: false          # don't run (show code only)
#| output: false        # hide output
#| warning: false       # hide warnings
#| include: false       # hide everything (no trace in output)
#| code-fold: true      # true | false — collapsible code block
#| code-summary: "Show the code"
#| fig-cap: "My figure"
#| fig-align: center    # left | center | right
#| label: fig-myplot    # for cross-referencing

Cross-reference labels



Type
Label prefix
Reference




Figure
fig-
@fig-myplot


Table
tbl-
@tbl-mydata


Section
sec- (on heading)
@sec-intro

title: Cheatsheet
text:
QMD — conditional content
::: {.content-visible when-profile="de"}
Nur auf Deutsch sichtbar.
:::

::: {.content-hidden when-format="pdf"}
Not in PDF.
:::

title: Cheatsheet
Cheatsheet
text:
Extensions — syntax summary

quizdown
```{quizdown}
---
shuffleAnswers: true
---
# Question text?
1. [x] Correct answer
2. [ ] Wrong answer
```


Pyodide
```{pyodide-python}
#| autorun: false
#| readonly: false
print("runs in browser")
```


py-exercise
```{py-exercise}
def my_func(x):
    pass        # student fills this in

## TESTS ##
assert my_func(2) == 4
```


JSXGraph
```jsxgraph
var board = JXG.JSXGraph.initBoard(BOARDID, {
    boundingbox: [-5, 5, 5, -5], axis: true
});
board.create('functiongraph', [Math.sin]);
```

title: Cheatsheet
text:
CSS — Bootstrap utility classes



Class
Effect




.text-center
Centre text


.text-muted

White text

Bold

Border

Rounded corners

Drop shadow

title: Cheatsheet
Multilanguage Projects
text: Quarto does not have a built-in translation system, but profiles make it straightforward to build a project that serves content in multiple languages — each profile renders to its own output folder.

title: Multilanguage Projects
text:
Project structure
my-project/
├── _quarto.yml
├── _quarto-de.yml
├── _quarto-en.yml
├── de/
│   ├── index.qmd
│   └── chapter1.qmd
├── en/
│   ├── index.qmd
│   └── chapter1.qmd
└── docs/
    ├── de/
    └── en/

title: Multilanguage Projects
text:
Step 1 — Profile group


_quarto.yml

project:
  type: website

profile:
  default: en
  group: [en, de]

title: Multilanguage Projects
text:
Step 2 — Per-language config files


_quarto-en.yml

project:
  output-dir: docs/en
  render:
    - en/*.qmd

website:
  title: "My Project"
  navbar:
    left:
      - text: "Home"
        href: en/index.qmd
      - text: "🇩🇪 Deutsch"
        href: "de/index.html"    # ← links to DE start page only



_quarto-de.yml

project:
  output-dir: docs/de
  render:
    - de/*.qmd

website:
  title: "Mein Projekt"
  navbar:
    left:
      - text: "Startseite"
        href: de/index.qmd
      - text: "🇬🇧 English"
        href: "en/index.html"    # ← links to EN start page only

title: Multilanguage Projects
text:
Step 3 — Render each language






ImportantQuarto cannot render all profiles at once natively



quarto render only runs the default profile. You must render each profile separately:
quarto render --profile en
quarto render --profile de
Use a build script (like preview.py in this project) to automate this.

title: Multilanguage Projects
text:
Language switcher fix
Quarto’s navbar links point to the other language’s homepage — they cannot know which page the reader is currently on.
A Python post-processing script solves this: after rendering, it rewrites the language-switch link in every HTML file to point to the equivalent page in the other language.
This works as long as your source files have matching names across languages:
en/chapter1.qmd  →  docs/en/chapter1.html
de/chapter1.qmd  →  docs/de/chapter1.html
Add this to your build script after rendering:
import os, re

def fix_language_links(docs_dir, lang_pairs):
    """
    Rewrite language-switch hrefs to point to the equivalent page
    instead of the other language's homepage.

    lang_pairs: list of (from_lang, to_lang) tuples, e.g. [("en", "de"), ("de", "en")]
    """
    docs_path = os.path.abspath(docs_dir)

    for from_lang, to_lang in lang_pairs:
        lang_dir = os.path.join(docs_path, from_lang)
        if not os.path.isdir(lang_dir):
            continue
        for dirpath, _, filenames in os.walk(lang_dir):
            for filename in filenames:
                if not filename.endswith(".html"):
                    continue
                html_file = os.path.join(dirpath, filename)
                rel = os.path.relpath(html_file, lang_dir)
                # Compute the path to the equivalent page in the other language
                depth = len(rel.replace("\\", "/").split("/")) - 1
                prefix = "../" * depth
                equivalent = prefix + "../" + to_lang + "/" + rel.replace("\\", "/")

                with open(html_file, encoding="utf-8") as f:
                    content = f.read()
                # Replace the generic language-switch link
                new_content = content.replace(
                    f'href="{prefix}../{to_lang}/index.html"',
                    f'href="{equivalent}"'
                )
                if new_content != content:
                    with open(html_file, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
                        f.write(new_content)

fix_language_links("docs", [("en", "de"), ("de", "en")])

title: Multilanguage Projects
section: Conditional content within a shared file
text:
Conditional content within a shared file
For small projects, you can maintain a single set of files and use conditional divs:
::: {.content-visible when-profile="en"}
Welcome to this tutorial.
:::

::: {.content-visible when-profile="de"}
Willkommen zu diesem Tutorial.
:::






Warning



This approach works for small projects but becomes hard to maintain as content grows. For anything beyond a few pages, separate source folders (one per language) are strongly recommended.

title: Multilanguage Projects
text:
Shared content across languages
Files that don’t need translation (code examples, shared figures) can be included with Quarto’s include shortcode:
{{< include ../../shared/code-example.qmd >}}
Place shared files in a shared/ folder outside the language folders.

title: The YML File
text: The _quarto.yml file is the control centre of your Quarto project. Everything you set here applies globally — unless overridden in a specific .qmd file or profile.

title: The YML File
text:
Output formats
Quarto can render the same source to multiple formats at once:


_quarto.yml

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
  pdf:
    toc: true
    documentclass: article
  docx:
    toc: true

Run all formats: quarto render Run one format: quarto render --to pdf






Tip



Any format option can be overridden per page in the .qmd file’s own YAML frontmatter.

title: The YML File
text:
Execute & freeze


_quarto.yml

execute:
  echo: true        # show source code
  eval: true        # run the code
  warning: false    # suppress warnings
  freeze: auto      # re-run only when source changes




freeze value
Behaviour




false
Always re-execute


true
Never re-execute — use cached output


auto
Re-execute only when the .qmd file changes



Frozen results are stored in _freeze/ — commit this folder so collaborators don’t need to re-run everything.






Warning



freeze: auto watches the .qmd file, not your data files. If you update a CSV but not the .qmd, delete the matching folder in _freeze/ to force a re-run.

title: The YML File
text:
Profiles
Profiles let you maintain multiple build variants — different audiences, languages, or formats — from one project.


_quarto.yml

profile:
  default: web
  group: [web, print]

Each profile has its own config file _quarto-<name>.yml that overrides or extends the root config:


_quarto-print.yml

project:
  output-dir: docs/print
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true

Render a specific profile:
quarto render --profile print






Tip



This entire tutorial is built with three profiles — beg, int, and exp — each with its own sidebar, navbar, and output folder.

title: The YML File
text:
Sidebar

Basic setup


_quarto.yml

website:
  sidebar:
    - id: my-sidebar
      style: docked       # docked | floating
      collapse-level: 1   # how many levels are expanded by default
      contents:
        - href: page.qmd
          text: "Page Title"



Sections — collapsible groups
Use section: to create collapsible groups in the sidebar. A section can have its own overview page (href) or just be a label:
contents:
  - section: "Chapter 1"          # label only, no page
    contents:
      - href: ch1/part1.qmd
        text: Part 1

  - section: "Chapter 2"
    href: ch2/index.qmd           # clicking the section title opens this page
    contents:
      - href: ch2/part1.qmd
        text: Part 1


Collapse level
collapse-level controls how many nesting levels are expanded when the page loads:



Value
Effect




1
Only top-level items visible, all sections collapsed


2
Top level and first-level sections expanded


3
Everything expanded



sidebar:
  collapse-level: 1   # start collapsed — reader expands what they need


Collapsible sidebar (hide/show)
To allow readers to collapse the sidebar entirely, use:


_quarto.yml

format:
  html:
    sidebar: true    # this is the default

For a floating sidebar that can be dismissed:
website:
  sidebar:
    style: floating

title: The YML File
